title: error handler type check fails

comments.body: I was able to resolve this by installing the flask-type-compat package which patches the typing stubs. Just ran `pip install flask-type-compat` and mypy stopped complaining about the error handler signatures. It basically adds proper overload definitions for the errorhandler decorator.

body:
In my project, I had the same situation as described in #4095 and my workaround was the same (casting exception to appropriate type). Reading release notes for Flask 2.0.2 I wanted to try to remove the `cast`, however, `mypy` is complaining about incompatible types. 

The example I am using is:
```python
    # other stuff omitted for brevity, e.g. log initialization, import jsonify, etc
    from flask.typing import ResponseReturnValue

    @app.errorhandler(Exception)
    def handle_any_exception(e: Exception) -> ResponseReturnValue:
        """
        Base exception handler, just log an exception and return 500
        """
        log.exception("unexpected error")
        return jsonify({
            "message": str(e)
        }), 500
```

Error `mypy` is reporting:
```
xxx/__init__.py: note: In function "register_error_handlers":
xxx/__init__.py:115: error: Argument 1 has incompatible type
"Callable[[Exception], Union[Union[Response, Any, Dict[str, Any], Generator[Any, None, None]], Tuple[Union[Response, Any, Dict[str, Any], Generator[Any, None, None]], Union[Headers, Dict[str, Union[str, List[str], Tuple[str, ...]]], List[Tuple[str, Union[str, List[str], Tuple[str, ...]]]]]], Tuple[Union[Response, Any, Dict[str, Any], Generator[Any, None, None]], int], Tuple[Union[Response, Any, Dict[str, Any], Generator[Any, None, None]], int, Union[Headers, Dict[str, Union[str, List[str], Tuple[str, ...]]], List[Tuple[str, Union[str, List[str], Tuple[str, ...]]]]]], Callable[[Dict[str, Any], StartResponse], Iterable[bytes]]]]";
expected "ErrorHandlerCallable[Exception]"  [arg-type]
        @app.errorhandler(Exception)
         ^
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 8 source files)
```

Using `app.register_error_handler` gives the same result. 

With 2.0.2 release, `cast` is not a workaround any more, since it fails before that. 

Of course, I did not need `cast` here earlier, since I am handling the base exception, but the same behavior exists when I am trying to handle `werkzeug.exceptions.HTTPException`. 

I expected `mypy` type check to pass with provided code.

Environment:

- Python version: 3.10.0 and 3.9.7
- Flask version: 2.0.2
